title: How to Apply for Bail
title_hi: ज़मानत (बेल) के लिए आवेदन कैसे करें
description: Step-by-step guide to bail application for bailable and non-bailable offences

steps.details: Check if the offence is BAILABLE or NON-BAILABLE. Bailable offences (less serious): Bail is a RIGHT — the police station itself must grant bail. Non-bailable offences (serious): Bail is at the court's discretion.

steps.action: For bailable offences: Apply at police station
steps.details: Under Section 436 CrPC, for bailable offences, the SHO must grant bail at the police station itself. You just need a surety (guarantor). If police refuse, approach the Magistrate who MUST grant bail.

steps.action: For non-bailable offences: File bail application in court
steps.details: Hire a lawyer and file a bail application under Section 437 CrPC in the court of the Magistrate. Include: details of the case, grounds for bail, accused's personal details, undertaking to cooperate.
If you apprehend arrest, apply for anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC in the Sessions Court or High Court. If granted, you won't be taken to jail upon arrest.
The judge will consider: nature of offence, evidence strength, flight risk, likelihood of tampering with evidence, accused's roots in community, health conditions.

steps.action: If bail is granted — fulfill conditions
steps.details: Bail conditions typically include: surrender passport, regular reporting to police station, not leaving jurisdiction, not contacting witnesses, furnishing surety bond.

steps.action: If bail is rejected — appeal to higher court
steps.details: Sessions Court rejection: Appeal to High Court under Section 439 CrPC. High Court rejection: Appeal to Supreme Court under Article 136/142.

required_documents: Employment proof (to show roots in community)
estimated_timeline: Bailable offence: same day. Non-bailable: 1-7 days. Anticipatory bail: 3-7 days.
tips: For bailable offences, bail is a RIGHT — police cannot refuse
You have the right to a free lawyer if you can't afford one (call 15100)
Default bail: If chargesheet is not filed within 60/90 days, you get bail automatically
Section 436A CrPC: If you've been in jail for half the maximum sentence, you must be released
Medical grounds can strengthen bail applications
Cooperating with investigation improves bail chances
